full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> I've shared with some of you in the past that several of us on staff, both past and present, have a special relationship with angels.<|13.66|> <|15.40|> We spent years touching the ceilings of our cars as we drove through the gates of Swan.<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Picking up our guardian angel as we left campus and dropping it back off as we returned.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> It's a time-honored tradition that students, alumni, and townsfolk observe.<|17.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is rare to be behind or passing another car at the gates where you don't see every<|9.84|> <|9.84|> occupant of that car reaching up to touch the ceiling.<|16.46|> <|16.46|> Tradition states that you are safe while you're on the mountain, and I know by Colorado standards.<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.30|> It's not a mountain.<|1.02|> <|2.12|> But this is Tennessee, and it's a capital M mountain, which is how they refer to the campus.<|8.56|> <|9.72|> But you must pick up your Suwannee angel as you leave through the gates and drop it back<|15.62|> <|15.62|> off upon your return.<|17.00|> <|17.00|> You are protected by this guardian angel while you are away from Suwannee.<|23.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.86|> It stays with you until you re-enter the gates and release it for much-needed rest.<|7.22|> <|8.16|> It's a sweet tradition, a lovely way to feel connected to a place that so many love so much<|16.62|> <|16.62|> and do indeed return to again and again.<|20.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.48|> And some of our angels probably have to work harder than others.<|5.02|> <|5.54|> I'm guessing our youth minister, Sarah Strand, gives hers a workout.<|9.76|> <|12.88|> And I'm sure there are those who think it's silly, but it is a flight of fancy that obliges.<|19.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> treasure. It is sentimental and imaginative in all the best ways. Sam Protaro, who is an<|9.96|> <|9.96|> Episcopal priest and has been a frequent visitor of St. John's Cathedral and is beloved here,<|15.96|> <|16.38|> has a lot to say about the role of angels and our imagination.<|21.94|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He contends that we too often fall prey to literalism in our life and in our faith.<|9.52|> <|10.92|> Angels help us guard against that and invite us to imagine realms we cannot see.<|19.18|> <|20.62|> He invites us to dream.<|22.14|> <|23.38|> These angels invite us to imagine.<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As we heard in our Old Testament reading,<|3.64|> <|3.64|> And Jacob dreamed that there was a ladder set up on the earth,<|9.52|> <|10.02|> the top of it reaching to heaven,<|12.12|> <|12.28|> and the angels of God were ascending and descending on it.<|16.64|> <|17.26|> And the Lord stood beside him and spoke to him in his dream about his promise.<|25.08|> <|25.08|> Amen.<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> His role in the covenant established with Abraham.<|4.38|> <|5.74|> This land would be his, and his messenger.<|24.38|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For Jacob, the angels signaled this visit and connected the earthly to the heavenly and the heavenly to the earthly, up and down and may assume human or non-human forms.<|14.08|> <|15.68|> Christians throughout time have felt themselves to be attended by helpful spirits.<|22.78|> <|22.78|> These spirits.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.92|> Swift, powerful, and enlightening.<|4.14|> <|5.14|> And these good spirits are often depicted in Christian art in human form,<|10.76|> <|10.76|> with wings to signify their swiftness and spacelessness,<|18.06|> <|18.86|> with swords to signify their power,<|21.88|> <|22.36|> and with dazzling garments to signify their ability to enlighten.<|27.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Unfortunately, this type of representation has led many to dismiss the angels as just<|9.40|> <|9.40|> another mythical beast, like a unicorn or a <|11.12|> praising, singing, and exhorting, flying about the throne of heaven.<|17.60|> <|18.76|> You cannot tell me that doesn't require some imagination.<|22.26|> <|22.26|> Amen.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Consider the next time you get a chance to go into St. Martin's, the angels depicted in the Reridos there behind the altar.<|10.94|> <|11.94|> Angels <|18.30|> only four are called by name.<|20.44|> <|21.24|> In this feast day of St. Michael and all angels,<|25.12|> <|25.12|> so Michael is one,<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Our friend Sam Portaro wonders, did Jacob mean this place upon where he stood?<|9.40|> <|10.30|> Or did he mean the place within himself, that wonderful place within the mind where the imagination lives,<|19.74|> <|20.46|> where we see angels, where we imagine a different world and a better one?<|26.94|> <|26.94|> Amen.<|27.60|>
